text: The surgeon places an epigastric port and uses suction to gently dissect and separate the omentum, revealing pus flakes. The distended gallbladder with gangrenous changes is visible as the colon and omentum are moved aside.
The surgeon uses a suction instrument to manage adhesions caused by cross inflammation during the laparoscopic cholecystectomy for gangrenous cholecystitis.
The surgeon uses a suction device to gently sweep away inflammatory adhesions, visibly separating them from surrounding tissues.
Following the separation of the omentum, the surgeon aspirates fluid from the peri-hepatic and sub-diaphragmatic regions using a suction device.
The surgeon separates the gallbladder from the omentum and colon, followed by the insertion of a 5mm working port for further laparoscopic access.
The surgeon positions a 5mm working port at the mid-clavicular line and another at the anterior axillary line to facilitate retraction of the gallbladder.
The surgeon uses suction to push the gallbladder back, assisted by another team member.
The surgeon uses tooth forceps to push the gallbladder back, facilitating further exposure and manipulation within the surgical field.
The surgeon completes the cholecystectomy using suction and a hook, with visible manipulation of the gallbladder and surrounding tissues.
The surgeon performs dissection using suction and a hook, while the assistant grasps the gallbladder and pushes it back for better exposure.
The surgeon attempts to grasp the gallbladder, which appears thickened and edematous, making the task challenging.
The surgeon punctures the gallbladder and aspirates the fluid within, facilitating easier dissection by reducing the organ's thickness and edema.

The surgeon uses the left hand to compress the duodenum and omentum while the right hand gently manipulates the gallbladder. The gallbladder is punctured at the site of gangrene, and pus is aspirated. The assistant grasps the gallbladder at the mid-body and pushes it back towards the right side to expose the neck.
The surgeon uses gentle suction to manipulate the gallbladder, aiding in the dissection process.
The surgeon creates a posterior window and lifts the gallbladder from the gallbladder bed, revealing the underlying anatomical structures.
The surgeon performs dissection of Calot's triangle using suction, with visible manipulation of the surrounding tissue.
The surgeon performs a gentle sweep movement around the gallbladder, avoiding excessive traction to prevent avulsion of vessels and potential bleeding.
The surgeon uses a suction device to perform sweeping movements, effectively opening up the Calot's triangle by clearing surrounding tissue.
The surgeon visually identifies the cystic artery during the cholecystectomy procedure, with the surrounding tissue exposed and visible.
The surgeon uses suction and the left-hand working port to gain access to Calot's triangle, facilitating the exposure of anatomical structures within this area.
With the neck of the gallbladder freed, the surgeon clearly delineates Calot's triangle, revealing two tubular structures entering the gallbladder.
The surgeon completes the dissection of Calot's triangle, revealing the cystic artery and other tubular structures entering the gallbladder.
The surgeon lifts two-thirds of the gallbladder from the gallbladder bed, revealing the underlying tissue and structures.
The surgeon applies a surgical clip to the cystic artery, securing it as part of the routine procedure during the cholecystectomy.
The surgeon uses gentle suction to clear the surgical field, managing edema in the inflamed tissue planes without the need for additional dissecting instruments.
The surgeon lifts the gallbladder and cystic duct, clearly defining the cystic duct's structure and its connection to the gallbladder.
After completing the dissection of Calot's triangle, the surgeon applies clips to the cystic duct, securing it for subsequent removal.
The surgical field is kept clear using suction before the clip applicator is introduced to apply clips to the cystic duct.

The surgeon compresses the bulging omentum using an assistant port on the left side to gain complete access. The cystic duct is cut, and the gallbladder is lifted from the gallbladder bed using suction. The upper clip does not fully occlude the cystic duct, but this is acceptable due to the gallbladder being punctured.

The surgeon dissects the gallbladder at the fundus using a hook, managing minor bleeding due to inflammation with compression and cautery. There is no significant bleeding observed during the procedure.

The surgeon uses a glove bag to retrieve the gallbladder specimen. A thorough lavage is performed in all quadrants, including the right upper quadrant, which is cleaned with saline. Clips are visible on the cystic artery and duct, and the surgical field is clear with no bleeding or ooze from the gallbladder bed.

The surgeon observes a grossly dilated colon due to gallbladder inflammation. The camera is positioned in the epigastric port, while suction is applied through the umbilical port to perform lavage. A drain is placed in the subhepatic space near the gallbladder fossa through the right-sided port.
The surgeon retrieves the gallbladder specimen using a glove bag through the epigastric port. The inflamed gallbladder is managed with suction to maintain a clear surgical field.
